query: recent studies on pelvic pain and defecatory symptoms in women with pelvic organ prolapse

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis, Pelvic Floor -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The major problems encountered in the anterior compartment include dysuria, urinary frequency, cystocele, and urinary incontinence. Vaginal or uterine prolapse usually represents central compartmental disorders. Changes in the posterior region cause pelvic and/or anal pain, constipation, rectal prolapse, and fecal incontinence. Peritoneal compartment disorders can lead to dyspareunia, constipation and low back pain due to alterations in rectum and sigma.

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Prolapse Imaging -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Pelvic floor prolapse is the herniation of the pelvic organs through the perineum. Depending on the pelvic organ involved, pelvic prolapse further categorizes into the anterior compartment containing urinary bladder(cystocele), the middle compartment containing uterine or vaginal prolapse (uterus or vagina), or the posterior compartment containing either the small bowel loops (enterocele) or rectum (rectocele). Pelvic prolapse is very common among multiparous women over 50, affecting approximately 50% of women over age 50. [1] The patients present with symptoms of stress fecal or urinary incontinence, uterine prolapse, constipation, or incomplete defecation. Besides, pelvic prolapse can negatively impact the patient's body image and sexuality. Pelvic prolapse treatments range from non-surgical approaches like Kegel exercise and pessary to various surgical procedures. [2] Treatments of pelvic prolapse significantly contribute to the healthcare cost in the United States, estimated at approximately $300 million from 2005 to 2006. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis, Pelvic Floor -- Clinical Significance -- The Prolapse of the Pelvic Organs, and Constipation
retrieved_docs.content: The prolapse of the pelvic organs caused by the weakness of the pelvic floor muscles is a problem affecting 30% to 50% of women, potentially leading to urinary and rectal problems, as well as disturbances to the sexual sphere. Researchers do not have accurate data on rectal prolapse in men. Constipation affects about 27% of the population, especially women, whose main causes can be related to the pelvic floor, to a paradoxical contraction of the musculature, general muscular weakness, and incomplete relaxation after contraction. Fecal accumulation in the rectum could lead to urinary and/or visceral prolapse.

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: As the symptoms and conditions of pelvic floor dysfunction span a broad spectrum of disciplines, determining the overall incidence of the amalgam of disorders is difficult. By the age of 80, about 11% of women will have one or more surgical interventions for urinary incontinence or pelvic organ prolapse. [13] [14] [15] Sexual dysfunction has been identified as a common problem involving up to 40% of reproductive-age women. [16] PFD can affect up to 50% of childbearing women. [17] Population-based survey studies have identified a lifetime prevalence of 17% to 19% for sexual pain disorders [2] Women with PFD concerns are more likely to report decreased arousal, infrequent orgasm, and increased dyspareunia. [18] [19] For women of childbearing age, PFD and POP are very common, and 65.8% of women over 40 years report at least one complaint of sexual dysfunction. [20] Interestingly, studies have not found sexual dysfunction to be directly related to PFD complaints of POP, stress urinary incontinence, overactive bladder, obstructed defecation, and fecal incontinence. [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: General Pelvic pain/pressure. Urologic Urinary hesitancy/frequency/urgency, dysuria, bladder pain, incontinence- urge, and stress. Gynecologic dyspareunia (during/after intercourse), sexual arousal, orgasm, bulging from the vagina. Colorectal Difficult/straining/incomplete evacuation of stool, bloating, constipation, fecal incontinence/leaking, prolapse/protrusion from anus. Splinting- pressure within the vagina or on the perineum to provide support and assist with voiding or defecation. [12] Voiding, defecation, pain, and dietary diaries are beneficial to assist with the evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Organ Prolapse -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A comprehensive evaluation for POP begins with a thorough medical, surgical, obstetric, and gynecological history. Particular attention should be given to symptoms of vaginal bulging and the extent to which these symptoms interfere with daily activities, sexual function, or show signs of progression over time, as many women with clinically apparent POP may be asymptomatic. [20] Evaluation should also include a detailed review of urinary function, with screening for stress or urgency incontinence, voiding difficulties, and compensatory behaviors such as manual splinting. Likewise, bowel habits should be evaluated for indications of posterior compartment involvement, including straining, incomplete evacuation, reliance on laxatives, or fecal incontinence.

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction -- Introduction -- Conditions
retrieved_docs.content: Gynecologic Dyspareunia: pain with or following sexual intercourse. Uterine prolapse: herniation of the uterus via the vagina beyond the introitus. Vaginal prolapse: herniation of the vaginal apex beyond the introitus. Enterocele: bulging or herniation of the intestines into the vagina (apical/posterior). Rectocele: bulging or herniation of the rectum into the vagina (posterior).

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Organ Prolapse -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Although pessary use is highly effective, patients should be counseled about potential complications before the pessary is placed. Follow-up symptoms such as vaginal discharge, irritation, ulceration, bleeding, pain, odor, vaginal wall ulceration, fistula formation, or bowel herniation may indicate pessary-related complications. [48] Infections with anaerobic organisms—particularly bacterial vaginosis—are more common in women who change their pessaries less than once per week. [49] Vaginal ulceration and bleeding are also more frequently observed in postmenopausal women and in those who remove their pessaries infrequently.

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of patients with PFD and POP concerns are initially directed toward their presenting complaints. As noted previously, the complaints and concerns often involve multiple systems requiring multidisciplinary care. Multiple forms of evaluation are available, but none are specifically diagnostic for PFD. Various tests are incorporated to augment the physician's clinical perception.

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Dynamic MRI: similar to defecography as the patient evacuates lubricating jelly that has been instilled into the rectum (no ionizing radiation; often non-physiologic defecation from a supine position).

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Organ Prolapse -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Effective management of POP often begins with a general obstetrician or gynecologist, who can manage low-grade cases. However, more advanced or complicated presentations, especially those involving comorbid conditions such as stress urinary incontinence, fecal incontinence, defecatory dysfunction, or other lower urinary tract abnormalities, may require referral to a urogynecologist and the involvement of a broader interprofessional healthcare team.

retrieved_docs.title: Uterine Prolapse(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Vaginal pessaries are objects often made of silicone that are inserted into the vagina to provide support for the prolapsed pelvic organs. It has been found that vaginal pessary has been a successful solution in 84% of cases of advanced pelvic organ prolapse, with mild adverse events in 31% of cases. [10] While pessaries do not reverse the herniation of pelvic organs, they can decrease symptoms and prevent the progression of prolapse. Patients must be fitted for a pessary and commonly try several pessaries before finding the appropriate one. Patients should have empty bowels and bladder when being fitted for a pessary. The examiner should be able to sweep a single finger between the pessary and vaginal walls. The patient should be able to walk, bend, and urinate comfortably without shifting the pessary. Complications of pessary placement include vaginal irritation/ulceration, discharge, pain, bleeding, and odor. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Organ Prolapse -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of POP integrates patient-reported symptoms with a standardized physical examination to accurately determine the type and severity of pelvic support defects. The assessment begins with a thorough history, including symptoms such as pelvic pressure or a sensation of vaginal bulge, urinary or fecal incontinence, voiding or defecatory dysfunction, and sexual discomfort. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Pelvic Organ Prolapse -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Pelvic organ prolapse (POP) is a prevalent condition that affects millions of women worldwide, particularly those with a history of vaginal childbirth, pelvic surgery, pelvic floor trauma, or connective tissue weakening. POP is characterized by the descent of pelvic organs, including the bladder, uterus, rectum, or vaginal apex, into or beyond the vaginal canal due to weakening of the supporting muscles, fascia, and ligaments. While some women remain asymptomatic, others may experience notable symptoms, including pelvic pressure, a sensation of vaginal bulging, urinary or fecal incontinence, and sexual dysfunction. Diagnosis relies on clinical history, pelvic examination, and standardized staging using either the Baden-Walker grading system or the Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification (POP-Q) system. Management is individualized based on symptom severity, compartment involvement, and patient goals. Conservative options include pelvic floor muscle training and pessary use, while surgical interventions, such as sacrocolpopexy or colpocleisis, may be indicated in more severe cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2631
retrieved_docs.content: Prolapse of female genital organs is a pelvic disorder that includes related subconditions and associations. It has a child manifestation known as تدلّي الإحليل, which refers to urethral prolapse, and it also has a relationship with تدلٍّ رحمي, indicating uterine prolapse. Recognizing that these entities are interconnected helps clinicians assess the full spectrum of pelvic organ prolapse in female patients and guides comprehensive evaluation and management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2550
retrieved_docs.content: Prolapse of female genital organs is a condition that encompasses a broader category of unspecified genital organ prolapse, referred to in Arabic as “تدلٍ غير محدد في الأعضاء التناسلية.” Within this broader context, a specific form of the condition is uterine prolapse, described in Arabic as “تدلٍّ رحمي.” Recognizing that uterine prolapse is a particular manifestation of the more general prolapse of female genital organs helps clinicians differentiate between generalized pelvic support failure and organ‑specific descent, thereby guiding appropriate diagnostic evaluation and targeted therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2532
retrieved_docs.content: Physicians – Obstetrics & Gynecology are specialists who perform salpingo‑oophorectomy, a surgical procedure that may be complete or partial and can be carried out unilaterally or bilaterally as a separate operation. In addition to this gynecologic surgery, these physicians also have expertise in the treatment of rectal prolapse (علاج قيلة مستقيمية), highlighting the breadth of their clinical scope across both reproductive‑system procedures and lower‑gastrointestinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2521
retrieved_docs.content: Herniated urinary bladder is classified as a form of تدلي الأعضاء التناسلية (genital organ prolapse). Within تدلي الأعضاء التناسلية, there are subtypes that include تدلٍّ رحمي مهبلي (uterovaginal prolapse) as well as تدلٍّ رحمي مهبلي، غير كامل (incomplete uterovaginal prolapse). This hierarchical relationship indicates that herniation of the urinary bladder represents one manifestation of genital organ prolapse, and that genital organ prolapse can further present as either a complete uterovaginal prolapse or an incomplete uterovaginal prolapse. Understanding these connections aids clinicians in recognizing the spectrum of pelvic organ prolapse conditions and tailoring appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 696
retrieved_docs.content: The disorder of female pelvic region and peritoneum is classified as a subtype of ناسور السبيل التناسلي لدى الإناث (female genital tract fistula). This fistula is located within the ženské pohlavní orgány (female genital organs). Dyspareunia due to non‑psychogenic cause in the female also has its finding site in the ženské pohlavní orgány, indicating that pain during intercourse can arise from pathology of these organs. Female genital and obstetric procedures are broadly related to the ženské pohlavní orgány, reflecting that surgical and obstetric interventions commonly involve this anatomical region. Together, these relationships emphasize the central role of the female genital organs in pelvic disorders, pain syndromes, and procedural care, highlighting the need for precise diagnosis and targeted management in female patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2570
retrieved_docs.content: Endometrial sampling (biopsy) with or without endocervical sampling (biopsy), without cervical dilation, any method (separate procedure) and excision of cervical stump by abdominal approach (procedure) are both clinical interventions that fall within the scope of Physicians – Obstetrics & Gynecology. Physicians – Obstetrics & Gynecology, as a specialty, is responsible for performing these gynecologic procedures, which are essential for diagnosing uterine pathology and managing post‑hysterectomy cervical remnants. In addition to these traditional gynecologic duties, the specialty of Physicians – Obstetrics & Gynecology is also associated with the management of علاج قيلة مستقيمية (treatment of rectal prolapse), reflecting a broader role in pelvic floor disorders where obstetric and gynecologic expertise can contribute to comprehensive patient care. Integrating these procedures and therapeutic areas under a single specialty underscores the importance of a multidisciplinary approach to women’s health and related pelvic conditions.
